answer:
**The work is *Gaṇapatitattva* (work slug: `unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136`), by an unknown author, and the rite is described at the end of its sole chapter, "Chapter 1."**[^1]

This chapter closes with the *paṅlukatan* (purification ritual) for Gaṇapati (also called Gaṇa), a son of Śiva, matching all specified details: the deity is depicted in a *rajah* (magical drawing) on *ivory bamboo (ampel gading)*, holding a discus in his left hand and a mace in his right; the ritual materials are a white-and-yellow *ajuman* set, a *sucī* offering with a white crested chicken as its main dish, water in a *copper vessel (sanku)*, *sudhāmala* flowers with *pras* and *santun* offerings, a monetary offering of 1100, and *samsam* of *katima* leaves.[^2] The rite's protections are effective against an epidemic, epilepsy, spirit affliction, and destruction caused by animals.[^3]

The requested invocation of the deity appears in verse 58:
> Destroyer of all poisons, Lord of Kāladṛṅga and Dṛṅgī (?),  
> who makes opposing diseases faint, who gives life to the three worlds.[^4]

[^1]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1 (`/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/`).
[^2]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1 (`/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-36c962`).
[^3]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1 (`/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-0dbd7d`).
[^4]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1 (`/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-a89bcc`).

args.query: Destroyer of all poisons, Lord of Kāladṛṅga and Dṛṅgī
